en.extract:
The little spiderhunter (Arachnothera longirostra) is a species of long-billed nectar-feeding bird in the family Nectariniidae found in the moist forests of South and Southeast Asia. Unlike typical sunbirds, males and females are very similar in plumage. They are usually seen in ones or twos and frequently make a tzeck call. They are most often found near flowering plants where they obtain nectar.


Description
The distinctive long beak set it apart from other sunbirds. The sexes are similar, except that the female has a paler base on the lower mandible. Male has all black beak. They are found near their favourite nectar bearing trees, often species of wild Musaceae or flowers in gardens. They have a buzzy zick-zick call that is made regularly when disturbed or when foraging. Additionally, the song is series of rapid chipping notes and these can go on for long periods.


Taxonomy

The genus name Arachnothera means "spider hunter" and the species epithet refers to the long beak. About thirteen geographic races are recognized. The nominate race longirostra (Latham, 1790) is found in southwestern India, central and northeastern India, foothills of the Himalayas and into Thailand and Yunnan. Race sordida La Touche, 1921 is found in southern China and northeastern Thailand. Race pallida Delacour, 1932 is found in eastern Thailand and C & S Indochina. Two species from the Philippines, the orange-tufted and the pale spiderhunter were formerly considered subspecies. Several other island forms are noted:

cinereicollis (Vieillot, 1819) – south of the Isthmus of Kra to Sumatra and satellite islands
zarhina Oberholser, 1912 – Banyak Is, off W Sumatra.
niasensis van Oort, 1910 – Nias I, off W Sumatra.
rothschildi van Oort, 1910 – N Natuna Is.
atita Oberholser, 1932 – S Natuna Is.
buettikoferi van Oort, 1910 – Borneo.
prillwitzi Hartert, 1901 – Java (including Madura I) and Bali
randi Salomonsen, 1953 – Basilan (off W Mindanao).


Distribution and habitat

The species is found in Bangladesh, Bhutan, Brunei, Cambodia, China, India, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Nepal, Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam. Within India, there are disjunct populations in the Eastern Ghats from Lamasinghi, Visakhapatnam and parts of Orissa) and the Western Ghats apart from the main distribution in north-east India that extends into Southeast Asia. A record from the Nicobar Islands is considered doubtful.
Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests, subtropical or tropical mangrove forests, and subtropical or tropical moist montane forests. It is usually found below the canopy. They are also found in gardens, attracted especially by flowers that yield nectar.


Behaviour and ecology

They have been noted as good pollinators of wild banana species and several species of the ginger family and often visit Loranthus sp. (= Dendrophthoe sp.), Bombax malabaricum and Erythrina indica for nectar. They are often seen in plantations in forest areas. Although they are more often seen in secondary forests or in clearings and appear to be tolerant of human activities, they have become extinct in some forest fragments. In Singapore they have gone locally extinct within the botanical garden.The breeding season in northeastern India is March to September but mainly May to August. In southern India it breeds from December to August. Two eggs are the usual clutch. The nest is a compact cup attached under a leaf of banana or similar broad leaved plant. The nest is hammock-like and suspended from the underside of a leaf using 150 or so "pop-rivets" of cobwebs and vegetable fibre, a unique method of using spider silk for animal architecture.  
It is suspected that Hodgson's hawk-cuckoo and violet cuckoo are brood parasites on this species in India.
A species of haemosporidian, Leucocytozoon, has been noted in specimens from Malaysia.


In culture
In Sarawak, the Kayan, Kenyah and Punan people consider it a bird of good omen and when they are out collecting camphor, the men would wait until they heard the sit call of one of these spiderhunters.


References


Other references
Rahman, M. A. 2006. Patterns of morphological variation in the little spiderhunter (Arachnothera longirostra) in Taman Negara, Peninsular Malaysia and Thale Ban National Park, Thailand. In: Othman, S., Yatim, S. H., Elagupillay, S., Md. Nor, S., Ahmad, N., and Mohd Sah, S. H (eds.), Pp: 207–214, Management and Status of Resources in Protected Areas for Peninsular Malaysia. Department of Wildlife and National Parks, Kuala Lumpur. ISBN 983-43010-0-6.
Khan, AR (1977): Territoriality among Sunbirds and Spiderhunter. Newsletter for Birdwatchers . 17(1), 5–6.
Law, SC (1945). "Note on the occurrence of some hitherto unrecorded birds in central and South Bengal". Ibis. 87 (3): 405–408. doi:10.1111/j.1474-919x.1945.tb01372.x.


External links

The '''little spiderhunter''' ('''''Arachnothera longirostra''''') is a species of long-billed nectar-feeding bird in the family [[Nectariniidae]] found in the moist forests of South and Southeast Asia. Unlike typical sunbirds, males and females are very similar in plumage. They are usually seen in ones or twos and frequently make a ''tzeck'' call. They are most often found near flowering plants where they obtain nectar.

==Description==
The distinctive long beak set it apart from other sunbirds. The sexes are similar, except that the female has a paler base on the lower mandible. Male has all black beak.<ref name=pcr>{{cite book|author1=Rasmussen PC  |author2=JC Anderton  |name-list-style=amp |year=2005| title= Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide. Volume 2| publisher= Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions|page=550}}</ref> They are found near their favourite nectar bearing trees, often species of wild Musaceae or flowers in gardens. They have a buzzy ''zick-zick'' call that is made regularly when disturbed or when foraging. Additionally, the song is series of rapid chipping notes and these can go on for long periods.<ref name=pcr/>

==Taxonomy==
[[File:Spiderhunter sipping honey.jpg|alt=Arachnothera longirostra|thumb|Little spiderhunter foraging on ''Musa'' sp.|left]]
The genus name ''Arachnothera'' means "spider hunter" and the species epithet refers to the long beak. About thirteen geographic races are recognized. The nominate race ''longirostra'' (Latham, 1790) is found in southwestern India, central and northeastern India, foothills of the Himalayas and into Thailand and Yunnan. Race ''sordida'' La Touche, 1921 is found in southern China and northeastern Thailand. Race ''pallida'' Delacour, 1932 is found in eastern Thailand and C & S Indochina. Two species from the Philippines, the [[Orange-tufted spiderhunter|orange-tufted]] and the [[pale spiderhunter]] were formerly considered subspecies. Several other island forms are noted:<ref>{{Cite journal| title=Patterns of genetic variation in the little spiderhunter (''Arachnothera longirostra'') in Southeast Asia| author1=Rahman, MA| author2=DFA Gawin| author3=C Moritz| journal=The Raffles Bulletin of Zoology| year=2010| volume=58| issue=2| pages=381–390| url=http://rmbr.nus.edu.sg/rbz/biblio/58/58rbz381-390.pdf| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140808070555/http://rmbr.nus.edu.sg/rbz/biblio/58/58rbz381-390.pdf| archive-date=2014-08-08}}</ref>
* ''cinereicollis'' (Vieillot, 1819) – south of the Isthmus of Kra to Sumatra and satellite islands
* ''zarhina'' Oberholser, 1912 – Banyak Is, off W Sumatra.
* ''niasensis'' van Oort, 1910 – Nias I, off W Sumatra.
* ''rothschildi'' van Oort, 1910 – N Natuna Is.
* ''atita'' Oberholser, 1932 – S Natuna Is.
* ''buettikoferi'' van Oort, 1910 – Borneo.
* ''prillwitzi'' Hartert, 1901 – Java (including Madura I) and Bali<ref>{{cite journal| title= Some notes on Java birds|journal= Novitates Zoologicae| volume=8| pages=49–53 |author=Hartert E |year= 1901 |url= https://archive.org/stream/novitateszoologi08lond#page/51/mode/1up/}}</ref>
* ''randi'' Salomonsen, 1953 – Basilan (off W Mindanao).

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Little_Spiderhunter_Nest.jpg|thumb|Nest under a leaf]]
The species is found in Bangladesh, Bhutan, Brunei, Cambodia, China, India, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Nepal, Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam. Within India, there are disjunct populations in the Eastern Ghats from Lamasinghi,<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Raju, KSR Krishna |author2=Selvin, Justus P |year=1971| title= Little Spiderhunter, ''Arachnothera longirostris'' (Latham) in the Eastern Ghats|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume= 68| issue=2|pages=454–455|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48430642}}</ref> Visakhapatnam and parts of Orissa<ref>{{cite journal|author= Majumdar, N |year=1979| title= New records of birds from Orissa| journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.  |volume=76| issue=1| pages=161–162|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48240522}}</ref>) and the Western Ghats apart from the main distribution in north-east India that extends into Southeast Asia.<ref name=hbk>{{cite book|author1=Ali, S  |author2=SD Ripley  |name-list-style=amp | year=1999| title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan. Volume 10|pages=55–56|publisher=Oxford University Press|edition=2nd}}</ref> A record from the Nicobar Islands is considered doubtful.<ref name=pcr/>

Its natural [[habitat]]s are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests, subtropical or tropical mangrove forests, and subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]]s. It is usually found below the canopy. They are also found in gardens, attracted especially by flowers that yield nectar.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1987.tb03201.x|author1=Beehler, B |author2=Raju, KSRK |author3=Ali, Shahid |year= 1987 |title= Avian use of man-disturbed forest in the Eastern Ghats, India| journal= Ibis |volume=129| issue=2| pages=197–211|doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
[[File:LittleSpiderhunterSong.ogg|thumb|left|Song of ''A. l. longirostra'', from Kerala, India]]
They have been noted as good pollinators of wild banana species<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Liu Ai-Zhong |author2=Li De-Zhu |author3=Hong Wang |author4=Kress W. John |year=2002| title= Ornithophilous and chiropterophilous pollination in ''Musa itinerans'' (musaceae), a pioneer species in tropical rain forests of Yunnan, Southwestern China|journal= Biotropica |volume=34|issue=2|pages=254–260|doi=10.1646/0006-3606(2002)034[0254:oacpim]2.0.co;2}}</ref> and several species of the ginger family<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/2656573|author1=Sakai, Shoko |author2=Kato, Makoto |author3=Inoue, Tamiji |year=1999| title=Three pollination guilds and variation in floral characteristics of Bornean gingers (Zingiberaceae and Costaceae)| journal=Am. J. Bot. |volume=86|issue=5|pages= 646–658 |jstor=2656573|pmid=10330067}}</ref> and often visit ''Loranthus'' sp. (= ''Dendrophthoe'' sp.),<ref>{{cite journal|vauthors=Devasahayam S, Rema J |year=1993 |title= Flower birds of Kozhikode District, Kerala |journal= Newsletter for Birdwatchers|volume=33 |issue=6 |pages=103–105|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW33_6#page/n8/mode/1up/}}</ref> ''[[Bombax malabaricum]]'' and ''[[Erythrina indica]]'' for nectar.<ref>{{cite book|vauthors=Devasahayam S, Rema J|year=1993 |chapter= Birds visiting flowers of Indian silk cotton tree (Bombax malabaricum) at Calicut, Kerala |title= Bird Conservation, Strategies for the Nineties and Beyond |veditors= Verghese A, Sridhar S, Chakravarthy AK |publisher= Ornithological Society of India, Bangalore |pages=184–185|chapter-url=https://archive.org/stream/BirdConservationStrategies/BirdConservationIndia#page/n211/mode/1up }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|vauthors=Devasahayam S, Rema J|year=2003 |title= Bird visitors to flowers of Indian coral tree (''Erythrina indica'' Lam.) at Kozhikode District, Kerala | journal= Newsletter for Birdwatchers |volume=43 |issue=5 |pages=72–73 }}</ref> They are often seen in plantations in forest areas.<ref>{{cite journal| doi=10.1007/BF03186376| author=Daniels, R J R| author2=Malati Hegde| author3=Madhav Gadgil| name-list-style=amp |year=1990| title= Birds of man-made ecosystems: the plantations| journal= Proceedings: Animal Sciences |volume=99|issue=1|pages=79–89| s2cid=129118660| url=https://archive.org/stream/indidanacademyof020239mbp#page/n82/mode/1up}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author= Ranganathan, J.|author2= R. J. Ranjit Daniels|author3= M. D. Subash Chandran|author4= Paul R. Ehrlich|author5= Gretchen C. Daily|name-list-style= amp  |year=2008| title= Sustaining biodiversity in ancient tropical countryside|journal=Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A.|pmid= 18981411|volume= 105| issue=46|pmc= 2577706|pages=17852–17854| doi=10.1073/pnas.0808874105|doi-access= free|bibcode= 2008PNAS..10517852R}}</ref> Although they are more often seen in secondary forests or in clearings and appear to be tolerant of human activities, they have become extinct in some forest fragments. In Singapore they have gone locally extinct within the botanical garden.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Navjot S. Sodhi |author2=Tien Ming Lee |author3=Lian Pin Koh |author4=Robert R. Dunn |name-list-style=amp |year=2005 |title=A century of avifaunal turnover in a small tropical rainforest fragment |journal=Animal Conservation |volume=8 |pages=217–222 |doi=10.1017/S1367943005001927 |url=http://www4.ncsu.edu/~rrdunn/Sodhi_et_al_2005_Anim_Con_8_217.pdf |issue=2 |bibcode=2005AnCon...8..217S |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080905003958/http://www4.ncsu.edu/~rrdunn/Sodhi_et_al_2005_Anim_Con_8_217.pdf |archive-date=2008-09-05 }}</ref>[[File:Egg spiderhunter.jpg|alt=Arachnothera longirostra egg|thumb|Little spiderhunter eggs<ref>{{cite journal|author=Baker, ECS|year=1901|title=On Indian birds' eggs and their variations|url=https://archive.org/stream/ibis14uniogoog#page/n470/mode/1up/|journal=Ibis|volume=8|issue=1|pages=411–423|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1901.tb00474.x}}</ref>]][[File:LittleSpiderhunter.ogg|thumb|left|Calls]]The breeding season in northeastern India is March to September but mainly May to August. In southern India it breeds from December to August. Two eggs are the usual clutch.<ref name="hbk" /> The nest is a compact cup attached under a leaf of banana or similar broad leaved plant. The nest is hammock-like and suspended from the underside of a leaf using 150 or so "pop-rivets" of cobwebs and vegetable fibre, a unique method of using spider silk for [[animal architecture]].<ref name="Hansell2007">{{cite book|last=Hansell|first=Michael Henry|title=Built by animals: the natural history of animal architecture|url=https://archive.org/details/builtbyanimalsna00hans|url-access=registration|access-date=1 July 2011|year=2007|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-920556-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/builtbyanimalsna00hans/page/19 19]–21}}</ref><!--Pages 19-21 not under preview.-->  
 
It is suspected that [[Hodgson's hawk-cuckoo]] and [[violet cuckoo]] are [[brood parasite]]s on this species in India.<ref>{{cite book|author1=Robert B. Payne |author2=Michael D. Sorenson |author3=Karen Klitz |year=2005|title= The Cuckoos: Cuculidae|url=https://archive.org/details/cuckoos00payn |url-access=limited |publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=0-19-850213-3|page=[https://archive.org/details/cuckoos00payn/page/n412 390]}}</ref>

A species of [[haemosporidia]]n, ''Leucocytozoon'', has been noted in specimens from Malaysia.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Paperna, Ilan |year=2008 |title=Haemosporozoan parasites found in birds in Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, Sarawak and Java |journal=The Raffles Bulletin of Zoology |volume=56 |issue=2 |pages=211–243 |url=http://rmbr.nus.edu.sg/rbz/biblio/56/56rbz211-243.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110606062258/http://rmbr.nus.edu.sg/rbz/biblio/56/56rbz211-243.pdf |archive-date=2011-06-06 }}</ref>

==In culture==
In Sarawak, the Kayan, Kenyah and Punan people consider it a bird of good omen and when they are out collecting [[camphor]], the men would wait until they heard the ''sit'' call of one of these spiderhunters.<ref>{{cite book|author=Haddon, AC| title=Head-hunters: Black, white and brown|year=1901| publisher=Methuen and Co., London |url=https://archive.org/stream/headhuntersblack00hadduoft#page/390/mode/1up/| pages=390–391}}</ref>

Department of Wildlife and National Parks, Kuala Lumpur. {{ISBN|983-43010-0-6}}.
* Khan, AR (1977): Territoriality among Sunbirds and Spiderhunter. ''[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]]'' . 17(1), 5–6.
* {{cite journal | last1 = Law | first1 = SC | year = 1945 | title = Note on the occurrence of some hitherto unrecorded birds in central and South Bengal | journal = Ibis | volume = 87 | issue = 3| pages = 405–408 | doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1945.tb01372.x}}

zh.extract:
长嘴捕蛛鸟（学名：Arachnothera longirostris）为太阳鸟科捕蛛鸟属的鸟类。其种加词“longirostris”意为“长吻的”。分布于印度经尼泊尔至印度、中南半岛、马来半岛、印度尼西亚、菲律宾以及中国大陆的云南等地，多生活于村寨附近阔叶树丛的小乔木树上。该物种的模式产地在孟加拉。


亚种
长嘴捕蛛鸟指名亚种（学名：Arachnothera longirostris longirostris）。分布于印度、尼泊尔至印度、缅甸、泰国以及中国大陆的云南等地。该物种的模式产地在孟加拉。
长嘴捕蛛鸟滇东亚种（学名：Arachnothera longirostris sordida）。分布于泰国、中南半岛以及中国大陆的云南等地。该物种的模式产地在云南河口。
